original_abstract: Understanding the interaction between nanocarriers and plasma proteins is vital for predicting their biological fate, yet the distinction between the "hard" and "soft" protein corona remains analytically challenging. In this study, we provide a complementary characterization of the protein corona formed on hydroxyethyl starch (HES) nanocapsules with various surface functionalizations. To investigate the hard corona, we utilized sodium dodecyl sulphate polyacrylamide gel electrophoresis (SDS-PAGE) and protein quantification assays, which revealed consistent adsorption patterns across different capsule types but showed a notable depletion of human serum albumin (HSA). In contrast, the soft corona was analyzed in situ using isothermal titration calorimetry (ITC) and dynamic light scattering (DLS). These techniques demonstrated that HSA actually adsorbs in high quantities but with low binding affinity, identifying it as a primary component of the soft corona. Conversely, apolipoprotein A-I (ApoA-I) exhibited high binding affinities despite low stoichiometric adsorption, confirming its role in the hard corona. Our findings highlight that the perceived composition of the protein corona is heavily dependent on the isolation and characterization techniques employed. We conclude that a multi-methodological approach is essential to accurately define the biological identity of nanomaterials for therapeutic applications.
humanized_abstract: Abstract This study aims to provide complementary information on the plasma protein corona surrounding hydroxyethyl starch (HES) nanocapsules, surface functionalized with different ligands, and shed light on the long-standing debate concerning the “hard” vs “soft” protein corona distinction. Through sodium dodecyl sulphate polyacrylamide gel electrophoresis (SDS-PAGE) and protein quantification assays, hard corona information was obtained, which showed a similar adsorption pattern for all the nanocapsules but depletion of human serum albumin (HSA). However, through isothermal titration calorimetry (ITC) and dynamic light scattering (DLS), performed in situ, the adsorption of a high amount of HSA with low binding affinity, pointed out its role as a major component of the soft corona. Meanwhile, apolipoprotein A-I (ApoA-I) shows high binding affinities, but low stoichiometry values, confirming its presence in the hard corona. The composition of the protein corona seems to greatly depend on the method used to isolate and analyze it. Therefore, it is essential to apply multiple approaches to determine the biological identity of nanoparticles in order to predict their behavior in biological media and ensure a successful therapeutic application.

Meanwhile, apolipoprotein A-I (ApoA-I) shows high binding affinities, but low stoichiometry values, confirming its presence in the hard corona. Therefore, it is essential to apply multiple approaches to determine the biological identity of nanoparticles in order to predict their behavior in biological media and ensure a successful therapeutic application.
